title: Contract Review Playbook
description: Schema for a contract-review playbook. One playbook governs the review of one agreement type. The schema is intentionally agreement-type-agnostic so the same engine can review ContractToasters, MSAs, NDAs, vendor agreements, etc.

agreement_type.description: Human-readable type, e.g. 'Educational Institution Affiliate Agreement'

agreement_aliases.description: Other names this agreement is called

exos_party.description: Default Exos legal entity for this agreement type

counterparty_type.description: What the other side is, e.g. 'Educational Institution'

based_on_corpus.description: Document IDs in our reference corpus this playbook was distilled from

status.description: SNAPSHOT LABEL ONLY — NOT the lifecycle authority. This field is written at upload time for human readability; it is a projection of playbook_versions.status (the DynamoDB row), which is the sole runtime lifecycle gate. Changing this field does NOT promote or retire a bundle; only the DB row does. Because this field is excluded from the canonical form (see 'canonicalization' at the schema root), flipping it draft→active does NOT change the content_hash, preserving the 'content-addressed immutable snapshot' guarantee. Semantics: draft = not yet approved; active = exactly one per playbook id, governs production reviews; retired = superseded, preserved for rollback lineage. A review must record the active version and content_hash at execution start; draft and retired playbooks must never govern a production review.

release.description: Signed release bundle metadata. Required before a playbook may become active. The release bundle binds the playbook, prompt, canonical standard form, anchor map, model policy, corpus snapshot, and evaluation run that Legal approved as one content-addressed unit. The anchor_map_hash field is required: a bundle cannot activate without it. NOTE: the release block and playbook.status are EXCLUDED from the canonical form used to compute content_hash — see 'canonicalization' property at the schema root for the normative definition.

content_hash.description: SHA-256 over the CANONICAL playbook content, prefixed 'sha256:'. The canonical form is the playbook JSON with 'playbook.status' and 'playbook.release' removed, serialized with sorted keys, no whitespace, UTF-8. This exclusion prevents circularity (the hash cannot include itself) and ensures the hash is stable under status flips (draft→active) and release-block population. Computed by scripts/canonicalize.py::content_hash(). Identifies the immutable snapshot; every review records this value. The activation gate (Gate 7) reads playbook_versions.content_hash and asserts it equals playbook_versions.legal_approval.content_hash before promotion.

snapshot_id.description: Content-addressed snapshot identifier in the playbook store (typically equals or derives from content_hash).

approved_by.description: Identity (Cognito sub or email) of the legal approver who authorized this release.

legal_approval.description: Explicit legal sign-off captured before activation. CI must verify this block exists and is internally consistent (see [docs/playbook-governance.md](../docs/playbook-governance.md)) before allowing status='active'.

approved.description: True only when a named legal owner has signed off on this exact content_hash.

approver.description: Legal owner of record for this release.

signature.description: Optional detached signature / KMS-signed token over content_hash for tamper-evidence.

supersedes.description: version this release replaces, for rollback/quarantine lineage.

standard_form_hash.description: SHA-256 over the canonical standard-form .docx paired with this playbook version.

prompt_hash.description: SHA-256 over the prompt/review-guidance artifact paired with this playbook version.

model_policy_hash.description: SHA-256 over the model-policy artifact that pins primary/critic model IDs, the embedding model ID, region, request contract (single-region native inference ID; geo and global inference profiles forbidden), and cost assumptions. A change to the embedding model is a model-policy change and forces a new model_policy_hash.

output_contract_hash.description: SHA-256 over playbooks/output-schema-v1.json (the versioned model-response schema). This binds the response shape to the release bundle: a change to the output schema — which defines what fields the model must emit and how they are validated — is a legal-output-affecting change and must force a new release bundle, just as a prompt or playbook change does. The CI subset-check verifies output_format.every_issue_includes ⊆ schema properties on every change. See docs/output-contract.md for the coupling rules.

corpus_snapshot_version.description: Active corpus snapshot approved for use with this release bundle.

eval_run_id.description: Evaluation run that passed schema, detector coverage, redline fixture, stochastic stability, retrieval, and leakage gates for this bundle.

anchor_map_hash.description: SHA-256 hash of the anchor-map artifact (standard-forms/<name>.anchor-map.json) derived from the bundled standard form at bundle-build time. This content-addresses the anchor map — including the §10 sub-clause splitting config — as a named release-bundle component. A change to the standard form's section structure (heading text, renumbering, sub-clause splits) forces a new anchor_map_hash and triggers the heading-hash drift gate in CI. Every review records this hash so anchor-map lineage is auditable.

decision_mode.description: External legal decision shape. Always 'binary' = ACCEPT | REQUEST_CHANGE only. Uncertainty and manual-review needs are NOT a third legal category; they are carried by the internal system_status (see output_format.system_status), which is distinct from the binary decision and never surfaced as a legal verdict.

primary_model_id.description: Pinned in-region Bedrock model ID for the primary reviewer. Global or geo inference IDs are rejected by CI unless explicitly approved.

critic_model_id.description: Pinned in-region Bedrock model ID for the adversarial critic. SHOULD be a DIFFERENT model from the primary so the two passes have decorrelated blind spots (and lower cost). v1 uses a Sonnet critic against an Opus primary.

embedding_model_id.description: Pinned in-region Bedrock embedding model ID used by the Knowledge Base. A change here (or any re-embedding) changes which precedents are retrieved and therefore legal output, so it is governed: recorded on every review, recertified quarterly, and requires admin (GC) approval. A re-embedding produces a new corpus_snapshot_version.

fallback_model_id.description: Optional pinned fallback model. Fallback use must be explicit, audited, and separately evaluated.

model_region.description: AWS region where inference runs, e.g. us-east-1.

request_contract.description: Named request-contract policy, including unsupported sampling params and extended-thinking constraints.

legal_approval.description: Standalone GC/legal sign-off record at the playbook level (distinct from release.legal_approval). Captures change-level approval for governance tracking; recorded when a GC authorizes a material edit outside of a full release bundle.

issue.description: GitHub issue or PR URL authorizing this change.

scope.description: Plain-English summary of what was approved.

approved_by.description: Name and title of the approver.

approved_commit.description: Git commit SHA at which approval was recorded.

general_principles.description: High-level constitution. Goes verbatim into the system prompt.

topics.description: Topic-by-topic positions. Each topic typically maps to a section or recurring clause. Topic 'id' values MUST be unique across the array and 'section_ref' values MUST be unique; CI enforces this (JSON Schema uniqueItems only de-dupes whole objects, so topic-coverage tests assert per-key uniqueness). Coverage rule: every section PRESENT in the canonical standard form maps to exactly one topic; topics that describe a position on a clause NOT in the standard form set 'not_in_standard': true, are exempt from the form-coverage check, but still need a unique 'section_ref'. See [docs/playbook-governance.md](../docs/playbook-governance.md) for the coverage and anchor gates.

id.description: Lowercase kebab-case slug, e.g. 'limitation-of-liability'. Unique across all topics; used as a stable cross-reference key from hard_rejections and from review output.

section_ref.description: Human-readable section number/title for display, e.g. '8 Limitation on Liability'. Unique across all topics (CI-enforced). This is a display label, not the machine key — see section_anchors.

section_anchors.description: Machine anchors (e.g. ['sec-8'], or ['sec-1.2','sec-1.4','sec-1.7'] for a topic spanning several clauses) into the canonical standard-form section-anchor map derived at bundle-build time from the standard-form .docx. The deterministic diff tags each hunk with its section_anchor; a hard_rejection scoped to this topic only reads hunks whose anchor is listed here. When not_in_standard is true, section_anchors MUST contain exactly ['sec-_new'] and nothing else. 'sec-_new' is a reserved pseudo-anchor (see sec-_new_anchor below): the diff tagger assigns it to any inserted hunk that does not fall inside an existing standard-form section. CI verifies every other anchor resolves to a real section of the bundled standard form; 'sec-_new' is exempt from that resolution check because it is a pseudo-anchor, not a real section.

not_in_standard.description: True when this topic states a position on a clause that is NOT present in the canonical standard form (e.g. indemnification, insurance, governing-law). Such topics MUST carry section_anchors: ['sec-_new'] (the reserved pseudo-anchor for wholly new inserted hunks — see sec-_new_anchor), are exempt from the form-coverage check, and may only be referenced by hard_rejections of kind 'on_insert' (you cannot remove a clause the standard form does not contain) — CI enforces this.

our_standard.description: Plain-English statement of our default position

requires_attorney_override.description: Issue #213: true when implementing this variation's 'to' text necessarily deletes or alters a protects.required_token guarded by an on_remove_or_alter hard_rejection scoped to this topic (e.g. raising a mutual liability cap changes the specific dollar figure a preserve-* Floor rule protects). Per playbooks/schema.json's hard_rejections[].protects description, numeric-threshold judgments ('is the new figure still acceptable') are deliberately NOT decided by the on_remove_or_alter detector -- that stays with attorney judgment. Marking a variation true here does NOT suppress or weaken the Floor: the deterministic layer still fires and forces REQUEST_CHANGE exactly as it does for any other protected-token change; the model can never auto-accept it. It documents that this specific, named acceptable outcome is reachable only via the attorney's disposition of that REQUEST_CHANGE (backend/src/disposition.py -- attorney_disposition is always recorded outside the tool), not via silent detector pass-through. tests/lint-acceptable-variations.py's on_remove_or_alter check (issue #213) treats a variation marked true as an intentional, documented exception from its zero-fire assertion (and fails if a marked variation does NOT actually fire anything, to catch stale markers) rather than a silent self-contradiction.

must_preserve.description: Elements of our position that cannot be removed regardless of other changes

reject_if_proposed.description: Specific deviations we will not accept

hard_rejection_refs.description: IDs of hard_rejections (see hard_rejections[].id) that apply to this topic. Required mapping: any topic whose section can carry an absolute line (e.g. indemnity, liability) MUST reference at least one hard_rejection id; CI's required-hard-rejection-mapping test enforces this for the configured high-risk topics.

replacement_text.description: Constraints on how the model may propose replacement language for this topic. Replacement text is a redline suggestion only and is bounded so the model cannot free-draft novel obligations.

mode.description: fixed = use 'fixed_text' verbatim; from_template = adapt a footnote/clause template; bounded_edit = minimal edit anchored to the existing clause; none = no replacement text permitted (flag only).

max_chars.description: Upper bound on proposed replacement length to prevent unbounded drafting.

must_not_introduce.description: Terms/concepts the replacement must never add (e.g. 'indemnify', 'consequential damages'), aligned with hard_rejections trigger_terms.

hard_rejections.description: Absolute lines we do not cross, applied across the topics they scope to. Active playbooks must use only structured detector-backed objects so the deterministic layer can force REQUEST_CHANGE without relying on semantic retrieval or model recall. CRITICAL: detectors run over the deterministic STANDARD-FORM DIFF (insertions / deletions / modifications anchored to standard-form sections), NEVER over raw full-document text. Running over raw text is a defect: most legal terms of art (e.g. 'limitation on liability', 'consequential damages', 'business associate', 'employee') appear in the canonical standard form itself and in fully-compliant drafts, so a raw-text matcher would force REQUEST_CHANGE on clean drafts that must ACCEPT. Each rule declares a 'kind': 'on_insert' (additive prohibition — reject if the counterparty INTRODUCES language) or 'on_remove_or_alter' (protective invariant — reject if the counterparty REMOVES/weakens a protection the standard form already provides). See [docs/playbook-governance.md](../docs/playbook-governance.md).

id.description: Unique kebab-case id, e.g. 'no-indemnity'. Referenced by topics[].hard_rejection_refs.

description.description: Human-readable statement of the line we don't cross.

kind.description: Discriminator that selects the detection mechanism. 'on_insert' = additive prohibition: trigger_terms are matched ONLY against inserted/modified-new diff spans (you cannot be punished for REMOVING a prohibited term). 'on_remove_or_alter' = protective invariant: fires when a required_token present in the standard side of the anchored section is deleted or materially altered on the counterparty side. CI enforces the conditional field requirements below.

trigger_terms.description: Required for kind='on_insert', forbidden for kind='on_remove_or_alter'. Exact lexical phrases the deterministic detector matches against INSERTED/MODIFIED-NEW diff spans only (never raw full text, never deleted text), e.g. 'indemnify', 'hold harmless', 'duty to defend', 'business associate', 'exclusive'. Case-insensitive, whole-phrase. A match within scope is a deterministic hard rejection.

match.description: How trigger_terms are matched (kind='on_insert' only). 'word_boundary' (default) = whole-phrase word-boundary match; 'substring' = case-insensitive substring; 'regex' = full Python re pattern applied to EVERY entry in trigger_terms. WARNING — ReDoS risk: 'regex' mode is an escape hatch for rules that cannot be expressed as phrase matches. Forbidden constructs (catastrophic backtracking, per issue #7 governance decision): nested unbounded quantifiers (X+)+, ambiguous alternation in unbounded repeat (a|ab)+, and unrestricted [^X]{n,} inside alternation. Every 'regex' trigger_term must complete in under 0.5 s on the adversarial corpus and pass the static structural check in tests/detector/test_regex_redos_guard.py. GC sign-off required on every 'regex' rule. See docs/playbook-governance.md § 'Regex-dialect and ReDoS constraint'. Setting match:'regex' applies to ALL of trigger_terms — a rule that needs to mix plain phrases with one regex pattern must NOT put the plain phrases in trigger_terms under match:'regex' (issue #220: this silently compiles plain phrases like 'hold harmless' as regex). Use regex_trigger_terms instead for the regex-only subset and leave trigger_terms/match on 'word_boundary' or 'substring' for the plain phrases.

regex_trigger_terms.description: Optional (kind='on_insert' only). Additional trigger phrases ALWAYS compiled as a full Python re pattern, regardless of this rule's 'match' value — the per-trigger-term escape hatch for a rule that needs to mix plain-phrase trigger_terms (matched per 'match', default word_boundary) with one or more regex patterns, without forcing every plain phrase in trigger_terms through the regex compiler (issue #220). Subject to the same ReDoS constraints as match:'regex' (forbidden constructs, 0.5s adversarial-corpus bound, static structural check, GC sign-off) — see docs/playbook-governance.md § 'Regex-dialect and ReDoS constraint'. A pattern here that fails to compile is a build failure, not a silent substring fallback.

match_surface.description: Which diff spans an 'on_insert' detector reads. 'inserted' = only counterparty-added runs; 'inserted_or_modified' = added runs plus the new side of a modification. Deleted (<w:del>) text is never scanned by an additive rule.

exempt_terms.description: Optional whole-phrase guards (kind='on_insert' only). A trigger hit that falls inside an exempt phrase does not fire — e.g. exempt_terms ['non-exclusive'] stops trigger 'exclusive' from firing on the compliant 'non-exclusive' language. Each exempt phrase must contain at least one trigger term (CI warns on dead config).

protects.description: Required for kind='on_remove_or_alter', forbidden for kind='on_insert'. Identifies the standard-form protection whose removal/alteration is a hard rejection. Detection is simple and diff-driven: because the review is a diff against a known standard form, a removal is directly visible. 'Material alteration' means a required_token present in the standard side of the anchored span is absent (deleted) or changed on the counterparty side; numeric-threshold judgments (e.g. 'cap below $50,000') are NOT decided here — they stay with the LLM review and topics[].reject_if_proposed.

section_anchor.description: Stable section anchor (see topics[].section_anchors) of the standard-form section that carries this protection. CI verifies it resolves to a real section of the bundled canonical standard form.

required_tokens.description: Load-bearing tokens of the protection (e.g. ['$150,000', 'aggregate liability']). CI verifies every token is actually PRESENT in this section of the canonical standard form (a protective rule guarding a token the standard does not contain is dead config and fails the build — this prevents the inverted-semantics bug).

token_policy.description: 'any' = removal/alteration of any required_token fires; 'all' = fires only if every required_token is gone.

fire_on.description: 'delete_only' = only outright deletion fires; 'delete_or_modify' = deletion OR a modification that removes/changes the token fires.

applies_to_topics.description: Topic ids this rejection scopes to. A detector only reads diff spans whose section_anchor is in the section_anchors of one of these topics. For on_insert rules scoped to not_in_standard topics, this includes the reserved pseudo-anchor 'sec-_new' (see sec-_new_anchor field in the schema root), which covers any inserted hunk that falls outside all existing standard-form sections. Omit for corpus-wide application (rare; discouraged for on_insert because it widens false-positive surface). CI verifies referential integrity: every id listed here must exist in topics[].id (both directions: topics[].hard_rejection_refs must resolve to hard_rejections[].id, and hard_rejections[].applies_to_topics must resolve to topics[].id). This is enforced by tests/test_schema_hardening.py and listed in docs/playbook-governance.md.

de_minimis_categories.description: Categories of change that are accepted even if technically novel.

decision_field.description: The shape of the external legal decision label. Binary only. Note the required attorney-disposition framing handled at render time (see [docs/output-contract.md](../docs/output-contract.md)): an ACCEPT is rendered as 'no requested changes identified by tool', NOT 'no action needed', and every output is watermarked 'tool recommendation only; attorney approval required'.

output_contract_version.description: The version string of the machine-readable response schema that governs both model passes for this playbook. Must match the 'output_contract_version' field in the referenced playbooks/output-schema-v1.json artifact (currently 'v1'). The full schema artifact is content-addressed by the release bundle's output_contract_hash. A change to the response schema shape must be delivered as a new schema version artifact and update this field. See docs/output-contract.md for the coupling rules.

every_issue_includes.description: The canonical list of fields every issue in the model response must carry. CI enforces that every field listed here is a top-level property of the issues[] object in the referenced output-schema-v1.json (every_issue_includes ⊆ schema properties). Keeping this list machine-readable (not just prose) makes the subset relationship enforceable without a code change.

citation_rules.description: Constraints on what external-facing footnotes/citations may reference. Enforced by the pre-render leakage scan (see [docs/output-contract.md](../docs/output-contract.md)). Footnotes shown to the counterparty must cite the CONTRACT POSITION (the section and our standard), never internal precedent.

external_footnotes_cite.description: External footnotes cite only the contract position / section reference and Exos standard. Counterparty name, deal date, and verbatim precedent language are NOT permitted in external footnotes.

precedent_citations.description: Any reference to corpus precedent (counterparty, date, executed-agreement language) is internal-audit-only and must be stripped from the generated .docx.

forbid_in_external_output.description: Phrasing/content classes the leakage scan blocks from external output, e.g. 'counterparty_name', 'precedent_deal_date', 'verbatim_precedent_text', 'system_prompt_fragment', 'internal_playbook_id'.

system_status.description: INTERNAL operational status, distinct from the binary legal decision. This carries pipeline confidence and manual-review routing as a SYSTEM STATE, never as a legal verdict. Low confidence yields REQUEST_CHANGE only when a concrete playbook issue exists; otherwise the review is routed to MANUAL_REVIEW as a system status. Structured-output failures map to ERROR_MANUAL_REVIEW_REQUIRED (after one bounded retry), kept distinct from transient pipeline errors.

states.description: Allowed internal status values. These never appear as the external decision_field.

confidence_field.description: Name of the internal confidence indicator emitted with each review, e.g. 'confidence' (qualitative band or score).

manual_review_routing.description: How a MANUAL_REVIEW status is routed; this is operational triage, not a legal category surfaced to the attorney as a third decision.

$schema.description: JSON Schema dialect reference. Allowed in playbook documents for tooling interop.

footnote_templates.description: Reusable rationale snippets the model can adapt for footnotes. Keyed by short slug.

coverage_exempt_anchors_NOTE.description: NOT a playbook field. Coverage exemptions are canonical in the anchor map (standard-forms/eiaa-v<version>.anchor-map.json), which carries 'coverage_exempt_anchors' and 'coverage_exempt_rationales' as siblings of 'anchors'. An exemption is a reviewed property of the standard-form section it describes (the section carries no reviewable legal clause), so it is governed alongside the form, not the playbook. The form-coverage gate (tests/anchor/test_form_coverage.py) reads exemptions from the anchor map only. This placeholder exists solely to document the single source of truth; it must not appear in a playbook instance.

anchor_migrations.description: Explicit records of anchor-to-heading-hash migrations across standard-form versions. Required whenever the canonical standard form is revised in a way that changes a section's heading text (including renumbering, which changes the heading). Each record documents the migration so the heading-hash drift CI gate can verify the change was intentional. An anchor whose heading hash changes without a covering migration record fails the drift gate. See RUNBOOK.md 'Revising the standard form' for the authoring procedure.

anchor.description: The stable anchor whose heading text changed.

from_heading_hash.description: SHA-256 of the heading text in the prior standard-form version.

to_heading_hash.description: SHA-256 of the heading text in the new standard-form version.

from_standard_form_hash.description: standard_form_hash of the prior release bundle.

to_standard_form_hash.description: standard_form_hash of the new release bundle.

reason.description: Why the heading text changed (e.g., 'section renumbered from §8 to §9 in v1.1 to accommodate new Insurance section').

approved_by.description: Identity (email or Cognito sub) of the legal approver for this migration.

sec-_new_anchor.description:
Normative definition of the reserved pseudo-anchor 'sec-_new'. This field is a schema-level definition block; it does not appear in playbook JSON instances. Playbook authors use 'sec-_new' in topics[].section_anchors; this block is the canonical specification of its semantics.

Purpose: the deterministic diff tagger assigns the pseudo-anchor 'sec-_new' to any diff hunk that represents a wholly NEW inserted section — one that does not fall inside any existing standard-form section. Without this pseudo-anchor, on_insert rules scoped to not_in_standard topics (which by definition have no section in the standard form) would have an empty effective hunk-scope and could never fire on the highest-risk insertions: standalone indemnification articles, excess insurance levels sections, counterparty-home arbitration clauses.

Semantics:
  - The diff tagger assigns anchor 'sec-_new' to any inserted hunk whose position in the document does not correspond to any section in the canonical standard-form section-anchor map.
  - 'sec-_new' is ONLY assigned to inserted/modified-new hunks; it is never assigned to deleted or unmodified hunks.
  - An on_insert rule whose applies_to_topics includes a topic with section_anchors: ['sec-_new'] will read any 'sec-_new' hunk in the diff.
  - Optional heading_keywords on the topic (not currently required) may further filter which sec-_new hunks a given topic's rules read, to reduce cross-topic false positives from unrelated new sections.

CI rules:
  - Every not_in_standard: true topic MUST carry section_anchors: ['sec-_new'] and nothing else. CI fails any not_in_standard topic with empty section_anchors (the dead-config bug) or with a real section anchor (which would violate the not_in_standard contract).
  - 'sec-_new' is exempt from the normal anchor-resolution check (CI does not verify it against the standard-form section-anchor map, since it is a pseudo-anchor with no corresponding standard-form section).
  - on_remove_or_alter rules MUST NOT reference 'sec-_new' (you cannot remove a clause the standard form never contained).

canonicalization.description:
Normative definition of the canonicalization rule used to compute release.content_hash. This field is a schema-level definition block; it does not appear in playbook JSON instances.

## Problem

The original schema described release.content_hash as 'SHA-256 over the canonicalized playbook content' without specifying the canonical form. Because the release block itself (containing content_hash, the approval signature, and supersedes) and playbook.status both live inside the playbook JSON, hashing the whole document is circular:
  - You need to know the hash before you can write it into release.content_hash.
  - Writing it changes the bytes you are hashing.
  - Flipping playbook.status from 'draft' to 'active' at promotion changes the bytes Legal approved.

## Canonical form (normative)

The canonical form is the playbook JSON with the following two keys REMOVED from the 'playbook' object before serialization:
  - playbook.status  — lifecycle state; playbook_versions.status (the DynamoDB row) is the sole authority.
  - playbook.release — the release bundle metadata block, which contains content_hash itself.

Serialization is deterministic:
  - Keys are sorted recursively (sort_keys=True in Python json.dumps).
  - No extra whitespace (separators=(',', ':')).
  - UTF-8 encoding.

The hash is 'sha256:' followed by the hex digest of SHA-256 over the UTF-8 bytes.

Implementation: scripts/canonicalize.py — functions canonicalize() and content_hash().
Golden-hash CI fixture: tests/gold-fixtures/canonicalize-golden-hash.json.

## Status authority (normative)

playbook_versions.status (the DynamoDB row) is the SOLE lifecycle authority.
playbook.status is a snapshot label written at upload time for human readability — a projection, never the runtime gate.

Consequence: promoting a bundle from draft to active does NOT require re-hashing or re-signing the playbook JSON. The content_hash computed at upload time is stable through the entire lifecycle.

## Gate 7 implementation

Gate 7 ('approved hashes match the artifacts being promoted') is implemented as follows:
  1. At upload time: content_hash(playbook_doc) is computed (via scripts/canonicalize.py) and stored in playbook_versions.content_hash.
  2. At legal-approval time: the approver records the exact content_hash they reviewed in playbook_versions.legal_approval.content_hash.
  3. At activation time: the CI / admin activation endpoint asserts:
       playbook_versions.content_hash == playbook_versions.legal_approval.content_hash
     If they differ, the bundle cannot be activated (the bytes changed after approval).
  4. The release.content_hash field in the JSON document is written at upload time so the document carries its own identity for audit — but activation reads the DB row, not the document field.
